title: Ischaemic Stroke
oneLiner: Acute focal neurological deficit due to arterial occlusion; common in older patients with vascular risk factors; rapid reperfusion and secondary prevention reduce disability and death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Haemorrhagic stroke (intracerebral / subarachnoid):** the single finding is hyperdensity on non-contrast CT head. *Never thrombolyse or start antiplatelets until haemorrhage is excluded.*
- **Seizure with Todd's paresis:** the finding is that the weakness resolves spontaneously over minutes to hours, and there is no acute infarct on MRI diffusion. *Treat the seizure first; do not assume ischaemic stroke if there is any seizure activity or post-ictal confusion.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** sudden-onset unilateral weakness (face, arm, leg), speech disturbance (dysphasia or dysarthria), unilateral sensory loss, visual field defect (homonymous hemianopia), ataxia/vertigo (posterior circulation)
- **Key exam findings:** FAST positive (Face, Arm, Speech, Time); arm drift, dysarthria or expressive/receptive dysphasia, pronator drift, visual inattention, gaze preference towards the side of the lesion, NIHSS score ≥1
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Haemorrhagic stroke** – CT shows hyperdensity; usually more severe headache, vomiting, reduced GCS
- **Transient ischaemic attack (TIA)** – symptoms resolve completely within 24 hours, no infarct on MRI diffusion; same initial workup as stroke
- **Migraine with aura** – younger patient, visual aura (scintillating scotomas, fortification spectra), headache, no fixed deficit; negative CT/MRI
- **Hypoglycaemia** – treatable cause of focal neurology; check capillary glucose immediately (fingerprick); resolves with IV dextrose
- **Sepsis / post-ictal weakness** – fever, source of infection, or witnessed seizure; no acute lesion on imaging
- **Functional (conversion) disorder** – non-anatomical weakness, inconsistency, Hoover's sign, normal imaging
- **Splitter:** blood on CT = haemorrhagic stroke; no blood + neurological deficit within 4.5 hours = candidate for thrombolysis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose (rule out hypo), ECG (AF, ischaemia), NIHSS score, NEWS2, 12‑lead ECG, oxygen saturations, BP
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, clotting screen (INR, APTT), troponin, glucose, lipids (fasting if possible), HbA1c, ESR (if young or vasculitis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** non-contrast CT head within 1 hour of arrival (rule out haemorrhage)
  - **Second-line:** CT angiography (if large vessel occlusion suspected and consideration for thrombectomy) or MRI brain with diffusion (gold standard for confirming acute infarction; can be delayed up to 24h if CT negative but high suspicion)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** diagnosis is clinical + imaging confirmation; no formal score required, but NIHSS used for severity stratification (0-42)
- **Never-miss test:** non-contrast CT head before any reperfusion therapy (thrombolysis or antiplatelet) – giving alteplase to a haemorrhagic stroke is potentially fatal
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - CT head to exclude haemorrhage
  - If within 4.5 hours of symptom onset and no contraindication → thrombolysis (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV) per NICE guideline
  - If large vessel occlusion on CTA and within 6-24 hours → discuss with regional thrombectomy centre for mechanical thrombectomy ([check local protocol])
  - Maintain BP <185/110 mmHg if thrombolysis candidate, otherwise permissive hypertension (do not lower unless >220/120 mmHg)
- **Ward (first 24-72 hours):**
  - Swallow screen before any oral intake (risk of aspiration)
  - If not thrombolysed: aspirin 300 mg loading dose (if haemorrhage excluded), then 300 mg OD for 2 weeks → then switch to clopidogrel 75 mg OD per NICE
  - If thrombolysed: no antiplatelet for 24 hours; repeat CT head before starting aspirin
  - Atorvastatin 80 mg OD (if no contraindication)
  - BP management: target <140/90 mmHg if not thrombolysed (but don't lower rapidly)
  - VTE prophylaxis: thromboembolic deterrent (TED) stockings; avoid pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in first 24 hours post-thrombolysis; if no bleed, consider LMWH [check local policy] after 48 hours if high risk
  - ECG monitoring for AF (continuous for at least 24 hours)
  - Stroke unit admission if available
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antiplatelet (clopidogrel 75 mg OD) or anticoagulation if AF (e.g. apixaban 5 mg BD; avoid warfarin due to variable INR)
  - BP control (target <140/90), statin (atorvastatin 80 mg), diabetes management
  - Lifestyle: smoking cessation, weight loss, exercise
  - Driving advice: must inform DVLA if residual deficit; no driving for 1 month after TIA, 6 months after stroke (or more depending on disability)
  - Carer and social support assessment
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride IV at 1 mL/kg/hour (or maintenance rate) until swallow assessed; avoid dextrose unless hypoglycaemic
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** TED stockings; no heparin/LMWH for first 48 hours post-thrombolysis; thereafter consider per local policy
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until swallow screen passed; then modified diet if dysphagia
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 4 hours; neuro observations (GCS, pupil size, limb power) every 1-4 hours depending on severity
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Alteplase (thrombolysis):** 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg) – give 10% as bolus over 1 minute, remainder as infusion over 60 minutes. Must be within 4.5 hours of onset ([check local protocol for extended window])
- **Aspirin (acute antiplatelet):** 300 mg PO/PR/NG OD for 2 weeks, then clopidogrel 75 mg OD
- **Clopidogrel (secondary prevention):** 75 mg OD (after 2 weeks of aspirin if acute)
- **Atorvastatin:** 80 mg OD
- **Antihypertensives:** permissive hypertension in acute phase; for BP >220/120 (or >185/110 if thrombolysis candidate) consider labetalol 10-20 mg IV or glyceryl trinitrate infusion ([check local protocol])
- **Key contraindications for alteplase:** recent major surgery/trauma, active bleeding, INR >1.7, platelets <100, BP >185/110, seizure at onset, suspected haemorrhage, recent head injury
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** alteplase – dose adjust no, but avoid if severe hepatic impairment; clopidogrel – no adjustment; atorvastatin – no adjustment; apixaban – reduce to 2.5 mg BD if age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, or creatinine ≥133 μmol/L
- **Most dangerous dose error:** giving alteplase before CT head excludes haemorrhage – always obtain CT first. Also, administering aspirin within 24 hours of thrombolysis increases haemorrhagic risk.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** haemorrhagic transformation (especially post-thrombolysis), malignant MCA syndrome (<10% of large territorial infarcts; cerebral oedema peaks at days 3-5), aspiration pneumonia, DVT/PE, seizures, urinary retention
- **Chronic:** post-stroke depression (up to 50%), spasticity, contractures, pressure sores, recurrent stroke/TIA
sections.Prognosis: - Early mortality ~10% (higher if large vessel occlusion, older age, high NIHSS). At 1 year, ~one third dead or dependent. Good recovery possible if rapid reperfusion; however, 25-30% remain significantly disabled. Early dysphagia is a strong predictor of poor outcome.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Screen for AF:** perform 24-hour Holter monitoring in cryptogenic stroke; consider extended patch monitor for paroxysmal AF
- **Swallow screen first:** before any oral intake – give the patient a sip of water and observe for cough, throat clearing, or voice change; if any concerns, keep NBM and refer to speech and language therapy
- **Start antiplatelet after 24 hours if thrombolysed:** repeat CT first; if no haemorrhagic transformation, then give aspirin 300 mg
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give antihypertensives in the first 24 hours unless BP is extremely high** (>220/120) or thrombolysis is planned – hypoperfusion of penumbra can worsen ischaemia
- **Do not delay CT for blood results** – priority is to exclude haemorrhage; bloods can be done simultaneously
- **Do not miss hypoglycaemia** – a single fingerprick glucose can save a patient from unnecessary thrombolysis; check immediately
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new or worsening weakness, new speech disturbance, new headache (especially thunderclap), seizure, difficulty swallowing, falls, or any signs of infection (fever, chest pain)
- **Follow-up:** stroke clinic within 2 weeks for TIA; within 1 month for stroke (or as per local protocol); monitor for post-stroke depression and carer fatigue
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / stroke team immediately if:** GCS drop ≥2 points, new focal deficit, seizure, BP >220/120 despite labetalol, NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4, or any suspected haemorrhagic transformation (e.g. sudden headache, vomiting, deterioration)
- **Call ICU / ITU if:** GCS ≤8, respiratory failure, refractory seizures, malignant MCA syndrome (pupil asymmetry, Cushing's response, brain herniation), or need for invasive monitoring

rawMarkdown:
## Ischaemic Stroke

**One-liner:** Acute focal neurological deficit due to arterial occlusion; common in older patients with vascular risk factors; rapid reperfusion and secondary prevention reduce disability and death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Haemorrhagic stroke (intracerebral / subarachnoid):** the single finding is hyperdensity on non-contrast CT head. *Never thrombolyse or start antiplatelets until haemorrhage is excluded.*
- **Seizure with Todd's paresis:** the finding is that the weakness resolves spontaneously over minutes to hours, and there is no acute infarct on MRI diffusion. *Treat the seizure first; do not assume ischaemic stroke if there is any seizure activity or post-ictal confusion.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** sudden-onset unilateral weakness (face, arm, leg), speech disturbance (dysphasia or dysarthria), unilateral sensory loss, visual field defect (homonymous hemianopia), ataxia/vertigo (posterior circulation)
- **Key exam findings:** FAST positive (Face, Arm, Speech, Time); arm drift, dysarthria or expressive/receptive dysphasia, pronator drift, visual inattention, gaze preference towards the side of the lesion, NIHSS score ≥1

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Haemorrhagic stroke** – CT shows hyperdensity; usually more severe headache, vomiting, reduced GCS
- **Transient ischaemic attack (TIA)** – symptoms resolve completely within 24 hours, no infarct on MRI diffusion; same initial workup as stroke
- **Migraine with aura** – younger patient, visual aura (scintillating scotomas, fortification spectra), headache, no fixed deficit; negative CT/MRI
- **Hypoglycaemia** – treatable cause of focal neurology; check capillary glucose immediately (fingerprick); resolves with IV dextrose
- **Sepsis / post-ictal weakness** – fever, source of infection, or witnessed seizure; no acute lesion on imaging
- **Functional (conversion) disorder** – non-anatomical weakness, inconsistency, Hoover's sign, normal imaging
- **Splitter:** blood on CT = haemorrhagic stroke; no blood + neurological deficit within 4.5 hours = candidate for thrombolysis

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose (rule out hypo), ECG (AF, ischaemia), NIHSS score, NEWS2, 12‑lead ECG, oxygen saturations, BP
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, clotting screen (INR, APTT), troponin, glucose, lipids (fasting if possible), HbA1c, ESR (if young or vasculitis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** non-contrast CT head within 1 hour of arrival (rule out haemorrhage)
  - **Second-line:** CT angiography (if large vessel occlusion suspected and consideration for thrombectomy) or MRI brain with diffusion (gold standard for confirming acute infarction; can be delayed up to 24h if CT negative but high suspicion)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** diagnosis is clinical + imaging confirmation; no formal score required, but NIHSS used for severity stratification (0-42)
- **Never-miss test:** non-contrast CT head before any reperfusion therapy (thrombolysis or antiplatelet) – giving alteplase to a haemorrhagic stroke is potentially fatal

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - CT head to exclude haemorrhage
  - If within 4.5 hours of symptom onset and no contraindication → thrombolysis (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV) per NICE guideline
  - If large vessel occlusion on CTA and within 6-24 hours → discuss with regional thrombectomy centre for mechanical thrombectomy ([check local protocol])
  - Maintain BP <185/110 mmHg if thrombolysis candidate, otherwise permissive hypertension (do not lower unless >220/120 mmHg)
- **Ward (first 24-72 hours):**
  - Swallow screen before any oral intake (risk of aspiration)
  - If not thrombolysed: aspirin 300 mg loading dose (if haemorrhage excluded), then 300 mg OD for 2 weeks → then switch to clopidogrel 75 mg OD per NICE
  - If thrombolysed: no antiplatelet for 24 hours; repeat CT head before starting aspirin
  - Atorvastatin 80 mg OD (if no contraindication)
  - BP management: target <140/90 mmHg if not thrombolysed (but don't lower rapidly)
  - VTE prophylaxis: thromboembolic deterrent (TED) stockings; avoid pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in first 24 hours post-thrombolysis; if no bleed, consider LMWH [check local policy] after 48 hours if high risk
  - ECG monitoring for AF (continuous for at least 24 hours)
  - Stroke unit admission if available
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antiplatelet (clopidogrel 75 mg OD) or anticoagulation if AF (e.g. apixaban 5 mg BD; avoid warfarin due to variable INR)
  - BP control (target <140/90), statin (atorvastatin 80 mg), diabetes management
  - Lifestyle: smoking cessation, weight loss, exercise
  - Driving advice: must inform DVLA if residual deficit; no driving for 1 month after TIA, 6 months after stroke (or more depending on disability)
  - Carer and social support assessment
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride IV at 1 mL/kg/hour (or maintenance rate) until swallow assessed; avoid dextrose unless hypoglycaemic
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** TED stockings; no heparin/LMWH for first 48 hours post-thrombolysis; thereafter consider per local policy
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until swallow screen passed; then modified diet if dysphagia
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 4 hours; neuro observations (GCS, pupil size, limb power) every 1-4 hours depending on severity

### Drugs & Doses
- **Alteplase (thrombolysis):** 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg) – give 10% as bolus over 1 minute, remainder as infusion over 60 minutes. Must be within 4.5 hours of onset ([check local protocol for extended window])
- **Aspirin (acute antiplatelet):** 300 mg PO/PR/NG OD for 2 weeks, then clopidogrel 75 mg OD
- **Clopidogrel (secondary prevention):** 75 mg OD (after 2 weeks of aspirin if acute)
- **Atorvastatin:** 80 mg OD
- **Antihypertensives:** permissive hypertension in acute phase; for BP >220/120 (or >185/110 if thrombolysis candidate) consider labetalol 10-20 mg IV or glyceryl trinitrate infusion ([check local protocol])
- **Key contraindications for alteplase:** recent major surgery/trauma, active bleeding, INR >1.7, platelets <100, BP >185/110, seizure at onset, suspected haemorrhage, recent head injury
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** alteplase – dose adjust no, but avoid if severe hepatic impairment; clopidogrel – no adjustment; atorvastatin – no adjustment; apixaban – reduce to 2.5 mg BD if age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, or creatinine ≥133 μmol/L
- **Most dangerous dose error:** giving alteplase before CT head excludes haemorrhage – always obtain CT first. Also, administering aspirin within 24 hours of thrombolysis increases haemorrhagic risk.

### Complications
- **Acute:** haemorrhagic transformation (especially post-thrombolysis), malignant MCA syndrome (<10% of large territorial infarcts; cerebral oedema peaks at days 3-5), aspiration pneumonia, DVT/PE, seizures, urinary retention
- **Chronic:** post-stroke depression (up to 50%), spasticity, contractures, pressure sores, recurrent stroke/TIA

### Prognosis
- Early mortality ~10% (higher if large vessel occlusion, older age, high NIHSS). At 1 year, ~one third dead or dependent. Good recovery possible if rapid reperfusion; however, 25-30% remain significantly disabled. Early dysphagia is a strong predictor of poor outcome.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Screen for AF:** perform 24-hour Holter monitoring in cryptogenic stroke; consider extended patch monitor for paroxysmal AF
- **Swallow screen first:** before any oral intake – give the patient a sip of water and observe for cough, throat clearing, or voice change; if any concerns, keep NBM and refer to speech and language therapy
- **Start antiplatelet after 24 hours if thrombolysed:** repeat CT first; if no haemorrhagic transformation, then give aspirin 300 mg

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give antihypertensives in the first 24 hours unless BP is extremely high** (>220/120) or thrombolysis is planned – hypoperfusion of penumbra can worsen ischaemia
- **Do not delay CT for blood results** – priority is to exclude haemorrhage; bloods can be done simultaneously
- **Do not miss hypoglycaemia** – a single fingerprick glucose can save a patient from unnecessary thrombolysis; check immediately

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new or worsening weakness, new speech disturbance, new headache (especially thunderclap), seizure, difficulty swallowing, falls, or any signs of infection (fever, chest pain)
- **Follow-up:** stroke clinic within 2 weeks for TIA; within 1 month for stroke (or as per local protocol); monitor for post-stroke depression and carer fatigue

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / stroke team immediately if:** GCS drop ≥2 points, new focal deficit, seizure, BP >220/120 despite labetalol, NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4, or any suspected haemorrhagic transformation (e.g. sudden headache, vomiting, deterioration)
- **Call ICU / ITU if:** GCS ≤8, respiratory failure, refractory seizures, malignant MCA syndrome (pupil asymmetry, Cushing's response, brain herniation), or need for invasive monitoring
